It will do so by providing guidance to coders on how to assign Type 1, 2, 3 or 4 status to qualitative text including, but not limited to, peer reviewed journal articles, course syllabi, policy statements, policy analyses, media communications and policy recommendations. This classification effort complements, rather than replaces, careful qualitative application of the framework required for uncovering important nuances through deliberative analysis and reflection of highly complex text. ***What the quantitative coding effort does well is to generate useful knowledge about whether the overall patterns identified by an one study or set of studies compare to other cases outside of the study. Coding also permits assessments as to whether there are differences in the dominance of one or more problem types across time and also space – be they countries, organizations, or sectors.*** Hence, not only can a well-developed code book generate descriptions about a range of policy and governance outcomes, it will also allow for broadening epistemological and methodological techniques that might explain variation – such as the correlation between the kind of disciplinary, sub-disciplinary and interdisciplinary training experts received that shape their bias in emphasizing one Type over the other. This in turn, might help generate knowledge about, and reverse when desired, “unconscious bias” about the way policy scholars and practitioners conceive of, and measure, “on the ground” problems. In other words, this framework can help advance “fit for purpose” policy analysis. To be clear: unlike Cashore’s broader argument about the drift away from Type 4 problems that he has, and is, applying to understand their role in shaping sustainability policy and governance over the last 35 years, the code book is designed to describe a range of outcomes, to which Cashore and his collaborators, and anyone else for that matter, can better assess, test, and develop their own arguments and theories. In short, the code book, paves the way for, but does not take a position on, which of the four problem types is preferred nor the causes of changes in emphasis of the Types. Importantly, although the 4PT distinctions originally came out of Cashore’s work on global, transnational and local approaches to sustainability policy and governance, they can be applied to disentangle how any kind of problem facing society and policy making are analysed, and treated, by scholars and experts. This means that ***while Cashore applies and draws examples from sustainability policy scholarship and practice, the framework is equally useful for anyone working on problem focused policy analysis including, but not limited to those scholars working in schools of public health, education, social work, forestry, fisheries, natural resources, agriculture, and even mining and energy for that matter.*** Having said this, the guidance and examples below for coders to be able to render concrete important but sometimes abstract distinctions, draw on examples from five years of efforts to code sustainability policy and governance analyses. This code book proceeds in the following steps. First, we discuss Cashore’s “utility” and “problem solving” labels as the two key distinctions that position the 4PT framework. Second, we review Cashore’s four key schools of sustainability thought and practice – including notable strands within them – to show how each of these 4PTs are subtly nurtured by sophisticated scholarly communities. Third, we review the challenges facing coding of different kinds of qualitative documents. Fourth, we review different sorting challenges based on the kinds of document being including abstracts of course syllabi, journal articles. Fifth we offer solutions to each including reviewing of our protocol for sorting scholarly articles through careful qualitative analysis that has been developed and revised over the last five years. We then review our approach to coding course syllabi and the development of key words to facilitate swift coding of a range of documents. We then discuss our systematic efforts to advance AI assisted coding with the goal of producing systematic text analysis that would be consistent with the laborious human coding efforts of the last six years (i.e. we seek to achieve intercoder reliability between AI and humans .8 or better). We conclude by offering recommendations on next steps for both Cashore’s team and collaborators, as well as those who might want to apply the 4PT framework beyond sustainability policy and governance considerations. Appendices A reviews our approach to coding articles beyond the two distinctions as a way to generate propositions about preliminary explanations for results and Appendix B which provides examples from cases with which to assist in sorting initially “ambiguous” cases. Section 2: ## a) Key Distinctions: Utility and Problem Solving Orientations The specific coding task appears at first glance seems quite straightforward i.e. to distinguish whether scholarly and practitioner policy analyses emphasise, or not, “utility” and “problem solving” orientations (Table 1). | Table1: The Four Problem Types (4PT)
Simplified Distinctions | Utility Dominates? YES | Utility Dominates? No | | ----------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------- | --------------------- | | **Problem Solving Dominates? YES** | TYPE 1 | TYPE 4 | | **Problem Solving Dominates? NO** | TYPE 2 | TYPE 3 | These two distinctions originally emerged from Cashore’s reflections, individually and with colleagues, about why so many sustainability policy experts have disagreed for over 35 years about how to think about, and also manage, critical environmental challenges facing the planet highlighted by climate, biodiversity and forest degradation. (Again, while derived from the sustainability policy and governance cases, the 4PT distinctions are meant to be applied to any “on the ground” problems facing policy makers and society including health care, education, child care, education and so on). The origins of the 4PT distinctions followed Cashore’s recognition, having only worked in interdisciplinary forestry, environment and policy schools, that longstanding largely unconscious biases among himself, his colleagues, and experts generally, about how to study the world of sustainability policy and governance, helped explain longstanding debates about not only about how to conceive of, and measure, sustainability challenges, but also recommended courses of policy action for managing the environment, including climate, species extinctions, biodiversity loss, as well as forests and natural resources management. What Cashore noticed was two key divides within these debates. ***The first distinction*** was whether sustainability scholars and practitioners believed that enhancing **“utility”** outcomes was the key task facing sustainability policy analysis. He noted that a large group of scholars generally felt that the more utility was enhanced, the better off society will be. While this causal belief is widely diffused among a range of analysts, it is also hotly debated by scholars and analysts who either oppose utility as a guiding orientation, and/or seek other ways to frame desired outcomes. Hence, Cashore recognized that the belief that utility enhancement was the preferable, from those that did not, was an excellent way to distinguish sustainability policy and governance analyses. ***Cashore’s second distinction*** came from his recognition that while some narrowed their conclusion to assessing how to solve or effectively manage the clearly specified problem at hand- such as such as saving a particular species from extinction, avoiding resource collapse of a specified fish stock, or limiting warming to 1.5 degrees above pre-industrial levels – others brought in analytical frames that allowed for a deliberations over not only how, but ***whether*** the problem at hand could or should be solved. That is, this latter group applied adjudicating frameworks that were developed as a way to address ***any*** particular problem – regardless as to its unique features - that might find its way to the societal or policy agenda in mind. Rather than generating research and analysis to shed light on how to manage a specific policy challenge, this group was united by developing frameworks that allow for consideration of not acting at all, or acting in a way that does not effectively manage the problem at hand. Section 3: ## b) Conceptual Foundations: Utility and Problem Contingent Labels While these distinctions showed great promise, the next task was to elaborate their meanings so that their influence could be better identified and assessed. Section 4: ### The utility label covers a broad range Cashore’s 4PT framework notes that the term “utility” is generally applied and defined broadly. It includes for example, those trained in welfare economics who, largely focusing on income or economic growth, convert others outcomes to “utility equivalents” in order to monetize, and compare, human preferences for green space and nature, with their direct interest in generating income. The term “utility” also incorporates the broader philosophy of “utilitarianism” that seeks to advance outcomes that produce the “greatest good for the greatest number in the long run”. It is also used more generally as a term to discuss whether, in terms of enhancing individual or collection satisfactions, there is any “utility” in a proposed course of action? For Cashore, the rationale for use of “utility” as a label was that it best captures the general belief behind a range of analyses that their goal is to improve “**human satisfactions”.** This is an important clarification because the utility as a term has a decidedly anthropogenetic orientation in which policy is geared towards improving the interests of humans either today or in the future. This, in turn, is shaped by some kind of homage and widespread work developing theories relevant to unpacking human **“self-interest”** .What is important for coding on the 4PT framework is that the concern itself is what is important – i.e. **the utility label in the 4PT framework is shorthand to capture those who are motivated to advance human self-interested satisfactions that incorporate a range of more specific satisfaction seeking rationales** This orientation in turn, allows coders to identify efforts to promote and understand **self-interested behaviours at multiple levels** of sustainability policy and governance including individual, organizational, nation state decisions as well as those within international governance arenas and global governance agreements. Hence, the utility label includes, for coding purposes, analyses focused on aggregating **individual utility** , **organizational utility** (such as companies returning shareholder value), or even **states’ utility** by maximizing their own self-interested preferences. This category incorporates, rather than distinguishes, longstanding debates among neo-realist and neo-liberal institutionalist scholars within international relations about whether self-interested, utility chasing states, are motivated to achieve relative or absolute gains. (The 4PT framework notes that debates about relative or absolute gains have undermine the similar quest to advance utility seeking interests, including, at the state level, relying on some conception of “national interest” that improves the satisfaction of its population through some kind of beneficial “rational” outcome. **Hence, this broad use of the utility label focuses coders on identifying any kind of analysis that is motivated by enhancing human satisfaction through self-interested preference maximization** . This allows coders to capture in one category a range of scholarship and analysis that although diverse and nuanced, are united by their underlying orientation in emphasizing individual, organizational and state based preferences that pay homage to improving human “satisfactions” at multiple levels. Section 5: ### The problem solving label: distinguishing analyses that target “how”, not “whether” --- The term “problem solving” is also used broadly to capture a range of expertise and approaches that undertake research with which to solve or effectively manage a particular “on the ground” problem. That is, they eschew asking “whether” a problem can be effectively managed, and instead ask, given the problem at hand, how can it be effectively managed? This category then incorporates a broad group of scholars and analysts who are united in their quest to understand and effectively management an “on the ground” problem such as the extinction of a particular species or limiting warming to no more than 1.5/2 °C above pre-industrial levels promoted through the Paris Accord. This means that the analyses they bring, and conclusions they make, will go back to implications for generating knowledge useful for problem solving of a problem, or a class of problems. Similarly, these analysts will never apply universal pre-established theories to assess whether the problem at hand ***ought*** to be solved. This is not their interest. Likewise while their research may carry lessons for other problems, they do not seek to generate universal theories that can be applied to any kind of problem. Put another way, this group of scholars would, for these reasons, avoid any quest for developing a theoretical orientation, or set of recommendations, that went beyond the problem or class of problems from which their research was derived. The practical result is that many in this category tend to carefully assess problem structure or “key features of the problem” they are attempting to shed light on how to better manage or solve. This is important. The reason they eschew any effort to develop a universal way to address any problem is because, by disconnecting from problem structure, the approach applied elsewhere might not effectively manage or solve another class of problems – i.e. it might even make those worse off. This means that they are largely conducting research in which its lessons are “problem contingent”. Any effort to generate lessons beyond a class of problems that is offered as a way to think about, and manage, all problems, is evidence that a scholarly analysis is not in this category. More specifically, any effort that concludes it is not “feasible” or “rational” to address a particular problem at hand that draws on a pre-established theory or framework that that was developed to analyse any and all problem is outside of this sorting category. This elaboration leads to the identification of a more nuanced table of which human and AI coders must be aware. Coders will note that I have also changed the Y axis label to “problem contingent” which I treat as synonymous as “problem solving” dominates, but also more useful for coding purposes. I keep the broader term in the first table as it is more accessible to the uninitiated coder, while bringing in the more specific term once they have read these first few overview pages. | Table 2: The Four Problem Types
Expanded Distinctions | Utility dominates: Does the analysis, conclusions, and theories treat individuals, organizations and states as largely self-interested, satisfaction driven entities that seek some kind “utility enhancing” outcome? YES | Utility dominates: Does the analysis, conclusions, and theories treat individuals, organizations and states as largely self-interested, satisfaction driven entities that seek some kind “utility enhancing” outcome? NO | | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | | **Problem contingent: Is the analysis, conclusions, and theories generated from, and narrowed to, understanding, improving, or solving, a clearly specified “on the ground” problem or class of problems? YES** | TYPE 1 Utility / Contingent | TYPE 4 Not Utility / Contingent | | **Problem contingent: Is the analysis, conclusions, and theories generated from, and narrowed to, understanding, improving, or solving, a clearly specified “on the ground” problem or class of problems? NO** | TYPE 2 Utility / Not Problem Contingent (Universal) | TYPE 3 Not Utility / Not Problem Contingent (Universal) | In sum, at the risk of repetition, let me be clear: these frameworks offer ways to deliberate over ***not only how, but whether, a specific problem can be effectively managed given a broader set of considerations beyond the problem*** itself. Section 6: ## C) Examples of Not Problem Contingent (Universal Approaches) As I discuss below I refer to the major strand of Optimization school of sustainability thought and practice known as “cost-benefit analysis” (CBA) as a leading example of this approach. This is because CBA frameworks assesses whether or not a policy intervention governing a particular problem will be able to produce, or not, aggregate utility benefits. Under this approach, if policy designers cannot find an intervention to address a problem at hand that will enhance overall utility (i.e. possible policy interventions are all deemed to undermine aggregate utility) then the “rational” answer from this CBA approach is not to act. There are, to be sure, many other strands of the Optimization school. For example, within international relations, both neo-realist and neo-liberal international relations scholars seek to generate theories of state preference, including longstanding debates about whether states maximize their preferences owing to absolute or relative gains. What is important for the 4PT framework is not their debates about underlying motivations that shape a given state’s “self-interest” preferences, but that both neo-realist and neo-liberal institutionalist scholars develop their theories and frameworks to explain ***state behaviour in general*** – i.e. they seek universal understandings of utility chasing state preference that explain, but are not derived from, their choices about how to manage a specified problem. That is, they develop and apply theories of state preferences that apply beyond a particular problem, but are meant to explain state behaviour over a particular problem. To repeat, their project is to develop universal theories of state action that are not derived from, or limited to, any specific problem or issue states happen to be deliberating over. These theories of state action also inform recommendations from these scholar about how states might act go address a specific problem in order to better achieve their pre-existing self-interested preferences (i.e. how to “optimize” outcomes given existing understandings of state preferences). This is why, as I discuss below, so many IR scholars make the case for addressing the global climate crisis based on the argument that doing so will advance state preferences, rather than asking whether a theory of state preferences is the most useful way to think about, and manage, particular kinds of problems. This same dilemma facing cost-benefit scholars who so believe in the utility enhancing motivations that they constantly argue, and work so hard to demonstrate – even despite overwhelming evidence to the contrary – that an emphasis on the utility undermining effects of the climate crisis is the most useful way to conceive of, and develop policy options for, averting global warming. As I discuss below, strands within the “Compromise School” of sustainability thought and practice are also replete with a range of efforts to develop universal theories of stakeholder engagement, legitimacy formation, and conflict resolution that, while rejecting utility chasing theories, nonetheless also develop frameworks for understanding motivations of individuals, organizations and states, that are also developed to understand, and prescribe solutions for, a range of problems that may come across the desk of policy officials. For example, there is one strand of the Compromise school known as “alternative dispute resolution” ADR that offers a way for stakeholder to agree on litigation avoiding natural resources management agreements that draws on universal theories of individual and organization behaviour as a way foster a compromise agreement among disparate interests. While drawing on theories that extend far beyond, and reject, utility motivations as far to narrow an approach to human behaviour, they share the same mission in developing university theories as a way to generate an understanding about not only how, but whether, the “on the ground” issue, or set of issues, can be resolved given the overall focus on conflict resolution rather than solving and effectively managing, the “on the ground” problem that spark the ADR in the first place. Another stand of the compromise school has been nurtured by those studying philosophy which seeks to generate a set of norms and protocols for making difficult decisions – such as whether to save someone stopped on the train tracks that might risk the lives of many on the train. In these cases, pre-existing approaches for developing normative theory based protocols for doing so – not based on utility per se but on claims to higher level “ethics” are turned to as a way to have society and policy makers thinks about managing difficult questions – from species extinctions that results from enhancing economic growth – to saving lives during a pandemic when the isolationist policies for doing say may undermine children’s cognitive development. In these cases what is important for the 4PT framework is that the adjudicating analysis that result are meant to help policy makers and society make decisions to address any problem that might come along, and are not derived, narrowed to, or contingent, on a particular class of problems. Section 7: ## d) Examples of Problem Contingent Approaches (Not Universal) In contrast, and as I elaborate below, members of Commons and Prioritization schools narrowing that adjudicating frameworks to only address, and are indeed derived from, a particular “on the ground” problem or class of problems – whether it be for utility enhancing (Commons) or non-utility (Prioritization) motivations. There are many strands within each of these schools that reinforce “problem contingent” rather than non-problem contingent universal (i.e.. meant to be applied to any problems) frameworks. As I discuss below the most famous utility seeking, problem contingent strand within sustainability studies are those seeking to understand, and effectively manage, “tragedies of the commons” that, if not well managed, produce avoidable resource collapse. Here, scholars seek to understand key features of the problem – such as Ostrom’s fundamental point that some commons tragedies – such as fish that swim - cannot be served by “excluding” access – and hence require different solutions to effectively manage them. The point for the “problem contingent” classification here is that scholars like Ostrom and her students who focus on “non-excludable” tragedies, only develop theories and approaches based on these key features. That is, they do not research, and have little to say, problems that do not share these key features. There are many likeminded strands about developing problem contingent approaches by those who do not share the Common’s utility orientation, but do share is focus on how to effectively manage specified “on the ground” problems – i.e. they eschew considerations about “whether” a specified problem can be solved, to deliberating over how it can solved or effectively managed. For example, Tribe’s work on species extinctions rejected analytical universal approaches offered by extant “policy sciences” framework, to show how any consideration to saving a particular species must begin, and end, based on the knowledge about what kind of conservation efforts is required to maintain the viability of that species. While Tribe and others like him turned to debates in philosophy and the study of “ethics”, he rejected as problematic any effort to address species extinctions that might produce an outcome that caused species extinctions. Hence universal adjudicating frames about particular protocols for assessing policy dilemmas – based on universal theories of “ethics”, was in fact, highly unethical. Others in this line of thinking within international relations have pointed to the “on the ground” problem of slavery. For this group, like species extinctions, the only way to eradicate slavery to develop policies and interventions based on achieving no-slavery. The idea of applying cost-benefitanalysis, or alternative dispute resolution, to assessing not only how, but whether, slavery can be eradicated is anathema to this strands of the prioritization school because it opens the door to consider policy options in which slavery might continue. Hence for these scholars, adjudicating frameworks are always derived from knowledge to manage a specified problems (or class of problems with similar structural features and attributes, such as Ostrom’s non-excludability tragedies). These distinctions have led Cashore to elaborate four categories to include these broader distinctions (Table 2) in which we can now further narrow “problem solving” to “problem contingent” which more accurately labels theories and frameworks and course of action that are never meant to be universal, but only applicable to a class of problems Section 8: ## e) Examples of the Resulting Four Problem Types (4PT) Now that we have covered how the 4PT framework defines utility and problem solving distinctions, and reviewed examples of universal versus problem contingent approaches, we are now in a position to identify examples of approaches classified within each: Type 1 (utility, problem contingent); Type 2 (utility, not problem contingent); Type 3 (not utility, not problem contingent); Type 4 (not utility, problem contingent). This section will, out of necessity, repeat some of the examples above. This is by design: repetition of examples will provide clarity when the same ones are used to distinguish different parts of the classification framework. In addition, this part is important since the coding is focused on the resulting four distinctions – so understanding how the above review figures for making decision about where to place a range of literatures is greatly facilitated by giving these concrete examples. This review, in turn, paves the way for a broader discussion of four key schools of sustainability that reinforce, but are also much broader, than the 4PTs. While broader, inclusion of discussion of the schools is expected to generate knowledge that will facilitate and enhance and generate a nuanced approach to coding the 4PTs. Section 9: ### i. Type 1: Utility Dominates, Problem Contingent **Type 1** then sorts those that are motivated by utility chasing orientations, but narrow their focus to a specified “on the ground” problem that is seen as undermining utility enhancement. Those scholars that are sorted into this category seek to generate analyses and ideas for intervening that improve utility outcomes for that problem. These approaches target the problem or class of problems they seek to ameliorate. Their interest is not in developing universal theories to address any and all sustainability problems. This means that they generate recommendations and analyses based on their problem or class of problems in isolation from other problems that might have different features. And they certainly would never apply a theoretical or analytical framework to discuss whether the problem should be solved. There are countless examples of this kind of orientation. For example, I have a colleague who studies the issue of taxis idling looking for riders in downtown Singapore when it appears that they would make more income (utility) by driving to the airport. This problem then can be solved by reducing “information asymmetry” though some kind of intervention – such as advertising or sharing this knowledge, or through some kind of regulation reducing an ability to idle. In this case, interventions are adjudicated only for their ability to manage that particular problem - regardless as to the possible effects of the “effective policy intervention” in reducing overall aggregate utility – such as those case in which limited government resources are better placed on managing another problem that would lead to higher utility. As I discussed above and elaborate below Type 1 sorting would also include a large group of scholars and practitioners working on “collective action theory” in general, and its application to sustainability through the “tragedy of the commons metaphor” in particular. This is an important distinction. Collective Action theorists are motivate by eliminating utility undermining outcomes such as rubber necking on highways, hording of toilet paper during pandemics, or over harvesting that leads to collapse of fish stocks or water supplies. These approaches, which draw on the classic “prisoners dilemma” in which, in the absence of an ability to develop and comply with, a collective agreements, two prisoners in different cells both have a “self-interest” to confess because if they do not, but their partner in crime does, they will be handed a much higher and longer prison sentence. This “confessing/confessing” strategy is considered to the most effective utility enhancing approach given no collective agreement (an outcome known as the “Nash equilibrium) but it is not the most utility enhancing solution. The most utility enhancing solution is for both not to confess, as this will lead to lower prison sentences overall. In this case, the issue then is how to build and design collective action institutions to allow for both not to confess. In the sustainability world, the issue then is how to build and design collective action institutions such that harvesting of the resource in question is less than or equal to the biological rate or reproduction. In all these cases, analyses are focused on human satisfactions, but only around the specific problem in question. As I discussed above and in more detail below, Elinor Ostrom’s path to earning the Nobel prize in economics was sparked by her recognition that for some utility undermining tragedies, highlighted by fish, excludability was not possible. This mean that traditional interventions to solve this utility undermining commons tragedies that turned to private property or state ownership, and that assumed excludability was possible, were ill suited to addressing those utility undermining resource depletion tragedies. Ostrom then turned to assessing how to effectively manage these “common pool resources” (CPRs) which she posited were better addressed through local resource governing innovations. The point for coders here is that all of these efforts paid attention to the key features of the problem at hand – specifically those contextual and contingent factors that explained the particular utility undermining outcome for the problem at hand. Collective action theorists in general, and commons tragedy scholars in particular, only generalize based on the specific problem at hand, or a class of problems such as those commons tragedies in which excludability is not possible. They would never therefore apply their lessons to managing all problems nor bringing in an assessment as to whether the problem can be solved. They only look at the how to do so, not whether. Section 10: ### ii. Type 2: Utility Dominates, Not Problem Contingent (Universal) In contrast, **Type 2** literatures share an interest in understanding and championing utility seeking preferences, but they reject a focus that only includes attention to the specific problem at hand. The logic behind this, whether CBA that targets and aggregates individual preferences, or IR theories that target and aggregate state preferences, is that that group believes that it is unwise to assess policy options for ameliorating a specified problem without considering, and finding a way to incorporate, the result of this decision on other kinds of problems. Their logic is clear: if solving or effectively managing one specific kind of utility undermining activity – such as say, over harvesting timber – results in a set of policy and governance decisions that might preclude more lucrative activities – such as say the introduction of copper mining and its promise of increase wages – then policy officials have undermined their responsibilities in managing for the greatest amount of utility with which to benefit the welfare of citizens. This category also captures the IR scholars above who also seek to generalize about the processes through which states do, or should, maximizing their preferences – often in the name of the “national interest”- that are then applied to assess how – and sometimes even whether – the problem at hand can be effectively managed given state preferences. Hence those analysts that turn to state preferences as a way to think about how to manage and develop policy response – whether it be, as discussed below, Nordhaus’ reasoning that optimal state responses to the utility undermining impacts of the climate crisis will produce a 3.2 degree world – in contrast to the 1.5 outcome a problem contingent approach of the IPCC. It also applied, as discussed below, to neo-liberal institutionalist Keohane whose climate research turns to his theory of state preferences for policy solutions, rather than interrogating the contingent nature of the climate crisis itself. Hence whether CBA, or conflict resolution or a host of other likeminded approaches, Type 4 classifies those analytical approaches that are not offered as contingent on solving a particular problem but as a way to adjudicate the problem at hand by apply a universal way to think about, measure, and identify, and project policy interventions. Hence, these approach there expands consideration, and offering a framework for doing so, about whether the problem at hand **can, or should,** be effectively managed based on some kind of approach to preference formation. Section 11: ### iii. Type 3: Utility Does Not Dominate, Not Problem Contingent (Universal) There are many diverse approaches that are nonetheless united by their **Type 3 status** : i.e. analytical frames for understanding, explaining and prescribing policy actions that generate protocols for doing so that eschew utility motivations as a dominant force or outcome, and instead reflect on a diverse set of motivations with which to understand, and prescribe courses of action. These approaches are united by rejecting the treatment of non-utility outcomes as “irrational” and instead wonder how to understand and incorporate cultural values, social practices, environmental norms, that produce some kind of way to both understand the world and also provide the basis for recommendations about how to include a diverse set of ideas, organizations that generate legitimate process and wide accepted outcomes. As reviewed above when discussing universal approaches, while they reject utility as a dominant motivation, they share the idea that pre-existing theories and frameworks can be applied to adjudicate not only how, but whether, a particular problem can be solved. In other words, with these approaches – whether originating from a particular set of ethical frames in philosophy, sociology, geography or critical theory, one never knows for sure whether applying a particular one of these approaches to a particular problem will result in the problem at hand being solved or effectively managed. In other words, in contrast to the teleological orientation of Types 1 and 4, Type 3 approaches work from a preexisting framework – or develop a framework from a case that is meant to apply to any chase – rather than developing a framework that only pertains to that problem or class of problems. Section 12: ### iv. Type 4: Utility Does Not Dominate, Problem Contingent For these reasons **Type 4** frameworks are distinguished, like Type 1, on their ability to help understand, and ameliorate, a specified problem from the Paris Accord’s 1.5/2 degree objectives, to a particular species being threatened with extinction. However unlike Type 1 reinforcing approaches, Type 4 problems not only reject utility as the guiding force, they usually find that when those chasing utility outcomes are effective, they usually cause, or exacerbate, a range of Type 4 problems. The classic example comes to us from the identification of two distinct problems that are outside of the sustainability challenges to which Cashore devoted his empirical work. The first problem is that of slavery – i.e. the act of one human having property rights over another human being – i.e. “owning” them. A range of scholarly analyses assessing this problem have found that the only way to ensure slavery is dramatically reduced or eradicated is to begin, and end, with the required outcome: i.e. no one, for any reason, is allowed to own another person. Many apply relevant frameworks have not only found that (Type 1 and 2) utility chasing motivations was behind slavery in the first place, but that application of Type 3, 2 frames permits deliberations over whether slavery ought to be considered – which, by definition, undermines an ability to achieve the desired outcome. The second example is anti-colonial norms in which reducing or averting an attempt by one country to take over another can only be achieved by teleological approaches that beginning, and end, with the absence of colonialism. Scholarly analysis reinforcing Type 4 hence would reject neo-liberal and neo-realist IR theories as permitting an understanding of state preferences that could also consider colonial type behaviours, rather than assessing mechanisms for ensuring these possibilities are never considered. As I discuss below, a number of analytical frames within environmental studies are consistent with Type 4 reasoning. Section 13: ## f) Coding Challenges The above review will most certainly facilitate, and place in context, efforts to code qualitative documents against the 4PTs. This facilitates, rather than overcomes, several challenges that a code book must incorporate. First, while some documents may seem straightforward, others may provide a nuanced or even contradictory account that makes sorting more challenging. The solution to this is to provide, and justify, systematic protocols to facilitate sorting of what we refer to as “ambiguous” cases below. Second, different types of documents will need slightly different ways to disentangle the 4PTs. For example, our careful review of journal articles on sustainability policy and governance requires laborious coding of highly complex articles. Our original focused on the Journal of Regulation and Governance was used to both revise our 4PT distinctions, and then to go back, recode, and assess intercoder reliability. However, our approach to coding abstracts for course syllabi at Yale’s environment schools was much swifter and faster – as we only needed to see whether the course championed a utility outcome or not, and whether it was focused on applying its approach to specific on the ground problems. Both coding exercises permitted the development of dominant “key words” within each both deductively and inductively. This means that one solution for accelerating coding through quantitively analysis of text is to identify the prevalence of different key words associated with one problem type over the others. Of course this approach is not perfect because sometimes some concepts such as “cost effectiveness” analysis can be used to achieve any kind of Type 4, 3 2 and 1 outcomes, while “cost benefit” reinforces Type 2. It may be difficult for using a key word approach alone to therefore uncover these nuances. Hence, while this approach may not be as nuanced as the human coding orientation, it promises to provide more swift data that itself, will be useful. And as along as standardizes approach is taken, it will still produce useful data even if that set might be slightly different than if laborious human coders had applied our more nuanced approach. Finally, and to integrate these different efforts, our ultimate goal is to draw on AI techniques that might best replicate human coding, but in much less time. Another challenges concerns whether an article is simply categorized by asking and answer the two distinguishing questions, or, to save, time, whether a sorting approach can be undertaken through a “branching tree” metaphore: i.e. first ask: “utility” if so, (1 or 2) then ask “problem contingent?, if so 1 or 4. Under this approach Type 3 emerges because other types are excluded, rather than affirmatively coding for Type 3. The problem with this approach – and which tripped us up initially – was that Type 3 was more likely to include ambiguous cases and or cases beyond our universe (i.e. they did not actually pertain to policy and governance). As a result, we had to regroup and instead encourage coders to identify each one according to the two categories, and then to note cases where they simply could not figure out how to best apply the code book (which meant we needed to revise and provide better guidance) and/or be clearer about whether a paper was in or out of our universe. Chertow Center for Industrial Ecology, School of Forestry and Environmental Studies, Yale University, New Haven, CT, USA Abstract Regulatory behavior and effectiveness in authoritarian settings are subject to alternative characterizations. By tracing enforce- ment processes through a variety of case studies, this article proposes and reﬁnes a new model, at least with respect to energy efﬁciency regulations in China: authoritarian but responsive. Local rulemaking and operationalization is authoritarian, with strong and coordinative bodies of regulation, strategic plans, and active involvement of local authorities. Local authorities, however, often ﬁnd themselves facing a welter of laws imposed on companies that create competing priorities for these local ofﬁcials who then must struggle to ﬁnd pragmatic solutions. On numerous occasions, such satisﬁcing behavior by local ofﬁ- cials makes them responsive to the performance and demands of regulated ﬁrms. Embedded in the decentralized authoritarian context, the authoritarian but responsive approach is found to be a rational choice of local governments and different from previous conceptualizations. It helps local governments coordinate across a diverse array of regulatory issues. Drawing on environmental enforcement in China, the model provides consistent explanations for the seemingly changing and discretion- ary enforcement incidents. Keywords: Chinese energy policy, decentralized authoritarianism, policy implementation, regulatory enforcement. 1. Introduction The need for effective implementation and enforcement of environmental policies is a common issue globally and of particular salience in rapidly industrializing economies. Environmental policies are essentially challenged by the fact that a ﬁrm may rationally choose to evade rather than comply if the evasion beneﬁts are found to out- weigh the potential costs of regulatory punishment (Gray & Shimshack 2011). Governments, too, may fail to enforce their own policies because of their institutional shortcomings (Schofer & Hironaka 2005). With increas- ing demand to reduce pollution and energy consumption, recent research in China indicates a regional variation in regulatory effectiveness. There is general improvement in implementation and enforcement across the country over time (van Rooij & Lo 2010; van Rooij et al. 2013), and coastal areas practice stronger enforcement than the inland west (Schwartz 2003; van Rooij & Lo 2010). Changing regulatory effectiveness is attributed to three different sources in the literature. The ﬁrst concerns institutional settings, such as the coming of “authoritarian environmentalism” (Beeson 2010), central government support (Tilt 2007; Zhan et al. 2014), and the convergence of multiple institutional forces (van Rooij & Lo 2010). The second focuses on the status of actors involved in the regulatory process, including the autonomy and strength of regulatory agencies (Schwartz 2003; Lo & Tang 2006; Liu et al. 2015), roles and perspectives of regula- tors (Chan et al. 1995; Lo et al. 2006; Eaton & Kostka 2014), bargaining power of regulated industries (Wang et al. 2003), and civil society support (Schwartz 2003; Tilt 2007; Tang et al. 2010; van Rooij et al. 2013). Finally, the third source draws on the speciﬁc strategies and styles that regulatory authorities use in enforcement, such as Correspondence: Marian R. While the explanations originate from seemingly different perspectives, scholars have started to link the ﬁrst example, institutional setting, with the third source, the behaviors of regulators. China’s institutional system is portrayed in the literature as having a dual nature. The often-stressed authoritarian system of top-down political control, limited participation, and paternalism is increasingly recognized and is the ﬁrst side of the duality (Landry 2008; Xu 2011). The other half that complements authoritarianism includes not only economic liberali- zation and resilience (Roy 1994; Nathan 2003), but also decentralization (Landry 2008, Xu 2011), fragmentation (Mertha 2009), consultation (Teets 2013), and even political deliberation (He & Warren 2011). The institutional setting shapes political and promotional incentives of local governments (Li & Zhou 2005), which leads both to their competition and policy experimentation (Heilmann 2008), and to their collaboration and collusion (Zhou 2010). Particularly for environmental and climate regulations, some authors argue that local governments take authoritarian, non-participatory approaches, which produce immediate policy outputs but not necessarily intended outcomes (Gilley 2012; Kostka 2015). This is accompanied by scholarly observations of plur- alized environmental actors (van Rooij et al. 2014) and their extra-legal behaviors intentionally ignored by the regulators (Weller 2012), as well as a call for broader, decentered regulation (Johnson 2016). This study takes a further step toward understanding the behavior and impact of local government in policy implementation, embedded in a regionally decentralized authoritarian regime. In contrast with considering enforcement as a one-time decision, we focus on the longer-term interactive process between regulators and regu- lated industrial ﬁrms in China, where demands for environmental protection and climate mitigation are an increasingly important dimension of local governments’ administrative concerns. By tracing the process of regula- tory interactions, we try to develop a more consistent, context-independent conceptualization of regulatory beha- viors and impacts. We trace the local implementation and enforcement process in the case of China’s energy efﬁciency policy based on interviews and archival documents from Jiangsu Province. The focus on a single but comprehensive reg- ulatory area helps us build and test our conceptualization model and explanations. Since the end of the 10th Five-Year Plan (FYP) period of 2005, the national and provincial governments of China have been seriously dedi- cated to reducing energy consumption per unit of gross domestic product (GDP) with punitive and exacting mea- sures. The policies have featured compulsory targets for national energy efﬁciency in the 11th and 12th FYPs, disaggregation and delegation of the targets through administrative ladders with spatial differences, and use of multiple policy instruments and energy saving measures (Zhou et al. 2010; Price et al. 2011; Zhu 2013). Jiangsu Province has one of the most developed economies and most stringent energy efﬁciency targets in China. Our ﬁndings indicate that local governments, although dominant in rulemaking and operationalization, also respond to the proposals, demands, and performance of regulated ﬁrms by preferentially lowering enforcement levels or alleviating previous punitive enforcement in cases when ﬁrms, attempting to respond to a welter of sometimes competing regulatory dicta, become willing to offer solutions that contribute back to local commu- nities and governance. The authoritarian but responsive approach is found both to lead to better understanding and cooperation by regulated ﬁrms and to improve government coordination across regulatory issues. Embedded in the institutional setting, the authoritarian but responsive approach is found here to be a rational and consistent choice by local government. As descriptive and explanatory rather than prescriptive, the authoritarian but responsive model we identiﬁed contributes to an understanding of regulatory behaviors and associated impacts within decentralized authoritarian settings. It connects the ideal of responsive regulation with institutional logic and reality in China, showing why and how the government responds and what differences it makes. Seemingly changing or differential enforcement follows a consistent rationale for better governance through responsiveness and coordination. This approach has advantages in local governance over the inﬂexible, top-down environmental authoritarianism that often creates ineffective policy outcomes (Gilley 2012; Kostka 2015) and highly variable enforcement styles selected by street- level bureaucrats whose actions confuse and subsequently undermine understanding and compliance by the regu- lated community (May & Wood 2003). It also differs from the theories of responsive authoritarianism (Weller 2012) and responsive regulation (Ayres & Braithwaite 1992; Parker 2013) in its distinctive scale and scope of reg- ulatory responsiveness. © 2017 John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd 2 J. Institutional background of energy efﬁciency policy Institutional settings shape the behaviors of regulatory agencies and particularly their responsiveness (Nielsen 2006). On the other hand, regulatory approaches may complement and strengthen the limited capacity of a regu- latory state (Braithwaite 2006). While normative arguments for certain regulatory approaches are well developed, there is also a necessary role for empirical investigation, especially for institutions undergoing rapid changes. Herein, we ﬁrst review the literature of institution and regulation, speciﬁcally regarding variation in enforcement, then introduce energy efﬁciency policies in China, and ﬁnally, propose our conceptualization for further develop- ment based on the literature and new empirical ﬁndings. 2.1. Institutional context and environmental regulation Scholars have described regulation in China as a regionally decentralized authoritarian system (Landry 2008; Xu 2011): it features a combination of centralized political and personnel controls, and regionally-based economic and administrative governance, where local governments compete with one another in economic development and policy reforms. The dual nature of the institution further manifests itself with respect to a few aspects of authoritarianism, including being formal rational, administratively competitive, fragmented, guided experimental, and responsive (van Rooij 2014). Environmental regulation operates within the decentralized authoritarian system. A theoretical approach that explains how governments in an authoritarian context respond to environmental challenges is authoritarian envi- ronmentalism. It is characterized by the primacy of capable, uncorrupted executive authority on improving environ- mental outcomes, technocratic decisionmaking, limited public participation, and suppressed individual liberty with regard to environmentally destructive behaviors (Beeson 2010; Gilley 2012). This model is considered to have both beneﬁts and shortcomings. The beneﬁts of authoritarian environmentalism include progressive policy response even under complex political pressures (Beeson 2010), resource mobilization, and power redistribution that facilitates policy implementation (Liu et al. 2015). On the negative side, rapid responses in policymaking may not necessarily lead to effective implementation around desired environmental outcomes (Gilley 2012), especially when frequent leadership turnovers encourage local governments to adopt shorter-term approaches (Eaton & Kostka 2014). Regional decentralization also serves as a basis for pluralized policy processes and varying enforcement. To be responsive to diverse demands, barriers to entry in agenda setting and policymaking are lowered (Wang 2008; Mertha 2009). In addition to direct regulators, actors engaged in the policy process may include business associa- tions (Yu et al. 2014), local media (Chin 2011), think tanks (Zhu 2009), and government ofﬁcials peripheral to the focused policies (Mertha 2009). In particular, environmental regulation features a broader incorporation of the court (Zhang 2016), prosecutors (Shi & van Rooij 2016), urban residents, and journalists (Johnson 2016). While the pluralized policy actors may explain part of the enforcement variation, the major factor is differ- ences among local regulatory authorities that are the dominant actors in regional economic and administrative governance. Differences in these authorities occur across jurisdictions in ﬁscal strength (Schwartz 2003), human resources (Schwartz 2003; Wheeler 2005), perception of enforced policies (Lo et al. 2006), environmental values (Chan et al. 1995), and the policies implemented in accord with local interests and policy priorities (Qi et al. 2008; Kostka & Hobbs 2012). These differences sometimes converge with other social and economic forces, and lead to variation in enforcement by regulatory authorities (van Rooij & Lo 2010). Previously, enforcement of centrally designed environmental policies was typically considered to be more or less half-hearted and ineffective because of the conﬂict between the economic costs of regulation and the local priority of economic growth. Recent energy efﬁciency policies, however, have been effectively implemented within the provinces without damaging the economy. The process of energy saving was achieved mainly through techno- logical change rather than industrial relocation: unlike the conventional wisdom that regulated industries would move out of areas of higher regulation, they invested substantially in more efﬁcient technologies instead (Zhu & Ruth 2015). The policies are reviewed further, followed by preliminary explanations. 2.2. Energy efﬁciency policies Energy conservation became part of the policy agenda in China in the 1980s. Since then, energy intensity, meas- ured as energy consumption per unit GDP, decreased for about 20 years, mainly driven by technological © 2017 John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd 3 Authoritarian but responsive J. Zhu and M. R. Chertow Page 4: upgrading (Zhang 2003; Ma & Stern 2008). This decline, however, was suddenly reversed during 2002–2004, creating the need for new policies. Facing these challenges, the central government established and reﬁned a com- prehensive and ambitious policy framework for the more efﬁcient use of energy, especially in the industrial sector. For research purposes, the post 2004 period became a distinctive boundary from which to measure policy-driven change. The policy framework consisted of general guidelines that proposed energy saving measures (Table A1), and detailed policies and programs to implement those measures (Table A2). General guidelines include: 1 Energy Conservation Law Amendments and the 11th and 12th Five-Year Plans for National Economic and Social Development ratiﬁed by the National People’s Congress. 2 Energy saving or climate mitigation decisions and plans announced by the State Council. 3 Speciﬁed plans for energy saving and climate mitigation in the industrial sector and longer-term plans designed by ministries in the State Council. The most important change called for the quantiﬁcation and application of compulsory targets for reducing national energy intensity by 20 percent between 2006 and 2010, and by another 16 percent between 2011 and 2015. The key regulatory agencies in policy design and implementation are the national and local Development and Reform Commissions, which are responsible for guiding economic and social development, and the Ministry of Industry and Information Technology and its local delegations, which are in charge of policies for individual industries. Collectively, these guidelines propose multiple industrial energy saving measures, including: target-setting for energy consumption per unit GDP, target disaggregation, and examination at subnational administrative levels; energy-efﬁciency performance standards for industrial processes and products; administration for ﬁrm-level energy-saving assessments, target-setting, planning, and supervision; promotion for energy-saving innovation, information, and technology adoption; industrial structural adjustment and elimination of outdated production capacity; and policy reforms to support energy saving (Zhu & Ruth 2015). Because industries feature different levels of energy intensity and excessive production capacity, more policy emphasis is attached to those industries with higher energy intensity and overcapacity in the guidelines. Detailed policies and programs were designed to focus on single or multiple energy saving measures, and sin- gle or multiple policy instruments. These instruments can be mandatory, voluntary, incentive-based, or derived from information disclosure. A major ﬁrm-level program, for example, is the Top-1000 Enterprise Energy Saving Program: ﬁrms with annual energy consumption beyond a threshold were required to join the program for energy saving, but their targets and planning for energy saving were proposed voluntarily and then negotiated with local governments. The program also incorporated information disclosure and incentives. The elimination of outdated production capacity became mandatory, providing information on which to base stricter enforcement specifying that facilities below certain technical standards must be retired following a timeline; the outdated facil- ities were charged with higher electricity prices – an incentive-based instrument – before retirement. 2.3. An authoritarian but responsive approach for energy efﬁciency? Energy efﬁciency policies have provided an opportunity for exploring the behavior of local government in policy implementation and enforcement within the regionally decentralized authoritarian regime (Kostka & Hobbs 2012; Eaton & Kostka 2014; Liu et al. 2015). Compared to traditional environmental issues, industrial energy efﬁ- ciency is of relatively less interest to nonconventional policy actors in China, such as the media and the public. Therefore, we can focus on the local regulatory authorities when studying policy enforcement. One can expect an authoritarian approach to local regulation for energy efﬁciency. The national policy design was determined in a short period with little participation from outside the government. Overall policy implemen- tation was manifested by the disaggregation and delegation of energy efﬁciency targets in a top-down manner through the administrative ladder. Fulﬁllment of the energy efﬁciency target was linked to top leaders’ evaluation and promotion in a region (Zhou et al. 2010), creating strong political motivation to ensure policy compliance. There is, however, also reason to anticipate a responsive part of the local regulation. The participation of non-regulatory actors has been observed as an important force in policymaking (Mertha 2009) and enforcement © 2017 John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd 4 J. Zhu and M. R. Chertow Authoritarian but responsive Page 5: in China (Schwartz 2003; van Rooij et al. 2013). Governments also intentionally leave more room for the media (Stockmann 2013) and civil society (Weller 2012), to respond to public demand (Chen et al. 2016), and to collab- orate with civil society to promote the delivery of public welfare (Teets 2013). Speciﬁcally, government policy choice is affected by ﬁrms’ strategic actions (Child & Tsai 2005; Zhu & Chertow 2017) and dominance in the local economy (Lorentzen et al. 2014), some of which is carried out by large energy consumers. We are interested not only in the existence and appearance of regulatory responsiveness, but also in whether it contributes to a better regulatory model in China, both prescriptively and descriptively. The authoritarian rule is complemented with deliberative practices, which is considered to affect political development (He & Warren 2011). As argued by Ayres and Braithwaite (1992), prescriptively, responsive regulation is a democratic ideal and has most often led to a dynamic process of enforcement and improved compliance by ﬁrms. In a descriptive and explanatory manner, we also expect to ﬁnd a more consistent rationale behind the seemingly random variation in individual enforcement incidents. 3. Methods To develop and reﬁne explanations of local government environmental policy enforcement behavior, we apply a case study framework for analytic generalization. The case studied is government enforcement of energy efﬁ- ciency policies in Jiangsu Province; the unit of analysis is an enforcement process at a single ﬁrm, which may involve regulatory authorities at different levels in Jiangsu and their interactions with the ﬁrm through time. Data were collected through interviews and archival documents of enforcement incidents in 21 ﬁrms from 2012 to 2015. Firm names and locations are coded to preserve anonymity. Analytic generalization is completed based on strategies of descriptive explanations and comparison across units of analysis (Yin 2014). Through this process, the preliminary explanation is revised and reﬁned to provide analytic explanations for government behaviors in policy enforcement. 3.1. Case selection Jiangsu Province was selected because it has the largest industrial sector among Chinese provinces, with well developed economic prosperity; its energy consumption per GDP was 29 percent lower than the national average in 2010 (People’s Government of Jiangsu 2012), but it was still one of the provinces with the largest percentage target for energy intensity reduction (State Council 2011). By considering a single province, we avoid the poten- tial variation among provinces that may complicate policy enforcement, for example in their individual cultures, interests, and priorities; their negotiation with the central government; and interpretation of the policies. Much variation may still exist for identifying and generalizing enforcement behaviors among municipalities and lower- level jurisdictions, especially between the more developed south and less developed north within the province. Energy efﬁciency became important in Jiangsu not only because of the policy itself, but also in the context of other policy targets. Two former Chinese Presidents, Jiang Zemin and Hu Jingtao, both required Jiangsu to be the ﬁrst province to fully build a “well-off society” (Xiaokang Shehui) and to complete modernization (People’s Government of Jiangsu 2014), two policy priorities of the central government with the former to be reached by 2020 and the latter by 2050 nationally. The goals of the provincial government, based on its self-designed sets of progressive qualiﬁcation standards, were to reach a well-off society in each county of the province by 2015 and modernization for the province as a whole by 2020 (China News 2011). Energy consumption per unit GDP is included in both sets of qualiﬁcations, at 0.062 kg of coal equivalent per RMB for the former and 0.05 kg of coal equivalent per RMB for the latter (Jiangsu Development and Reform Commission 2013a,b). Because the govern- ment of Jiangsu Province has both strong motivation and a social and economic basis to seriously enforce the policy, this enables a focus on the interacting behavior in policy enforcement within the context of decentralized authoritarianism. We focus on enforcement in four municipalities in Jiangsu Province, characterized in Table 1 with a qualita- tive comparison that prevents their identities from being revealed. Three are in the south of the province with highly industrialized or post-industrialized economies, a high level of socioeconomic development, and strong political interest in reducing overall energy intensity within their jurisdictions. Chertow Page 6: only helps local administrative leaders with their career evaluation directly, but also indirectly as an important qualifying condition to move regions under their jurisdictions ahead of the provincial agenda for the “two policy priorities.” To improve comparison and generalization, one municipality in the north was also selected, with a relatively lower level of socioeconomic development and a lower priority for energy efﬁciency. The municipalities may vary in their own policy agendas and administrative approaches. Analytic generalization requires enforcement incidents to be selected based on theoretical replication, rather than representative sampling (Yin 2014). The incidents for analyses, therefore, were carefully selected to highlight contrasting contextual and internal factors that may affect interaction between the regulators and the regulated. Characteristics to be considered for the regulated include industrial sector, ﬁrm size, ownership, and age. We focus on policy implementation and enforcement in four industries: cement, iron and steel, paper, and chemical products (Table 2). The industries feature different national-level and provincial-level regulations. Cement and steel industries, both experiencing serious overcapacity, have been strictly mandated to eliminate production facilities, especially those using certain technologies and under certain capacities, and then must stay below a capacity cap in each region. There are some requirements for speciﬁc facility elimination in the paper industry, but no explicit constraint for production capacity. The chemical industry has been required to eliminate outdated facilities, but only for a few processes that experience overcapacity, such as the chloral-alkali process. Firm size, ownership, and age may also affect the enforcement process. Larger ﬁrms are expected to have greater capability for energy saving, be more visible in enforcement, but also to have greater bargaining power. Data collection and analysis Data of both the regulators and the regulated ﬁrms were collected mainly from two sources – archival documents and in-depth interviews – from 2012 to 2015. For the regulators, the main focus is local government subsidiaries in charge of industrial energy efﬁciency – the economic and information technology commissions, as well as development and reform commissions and environmental protection bureaus that also play different roles in managing industrial energy efﬁciency. Government websites and media provided general descriptions of the interpretation and implementation of energy efﬁciency and related policies by provincial and local authorities. The information was ﬁrst collected by searching the websites and through search engines according to the key words of the policy programs listed in the Appendix. A thorough review of the online archives of the regulatory authorities followed, seeking documents directly related to energy efﬁciency regulation at the provincial and local levels. Open-ended interviews were conducted with two provincial and nine local ofﬁcials in charge of energy sav- ing and related areas to elicit their comments on major policies, policy interpretation, implementation, and effec- tiveness. The interviews were conducted in their own private ofﬁces. With respect to the entities being regulated, background information of the selected ﬁrms was ﬁrst collected in a screening process through their websites, media coverage, and government archives. Thirty-two semi- structured interviews were conducted at 21 ﬁrms, with their chief executives, directors in charge of production, energy management, or environment and safety personnel. Twenty-three interviews were permitted to be audio- recorded, and detailed notes were taken for the rest. Interviews were all conducted in Mandarin Chinese and usu- ally lasted for one hour in the interviewees’ ofﬁces or meeting rooms. An interviewee was ﬁrst asked to brieﬂy introduce the ﬁrm, its position in the industry, and detail all of its past energy saving initiatives. Our main focus was enforcement incidents at cement and steel ﬁrms, which were embedded in more diverse contexts and were under stricter policies for energy efﬁciency (Table 3). 4. Findings We identiﬁed authoritarian rulemaking and operationalization by local governments that oversee industrial energy efﬁciency, followed by responsiveness in individual instances of enforcement according to ﬁrms’ performance and proposals, which led to stringent but ﬂexible enforcement. The scope of responsiveness varied across municipalities and through time, reﬂecting governments’ coordination among locally important, but sometimes overlapping areas of governance. The general mechanisms are illustrated in Figure 1, and are explained in more detail. 4.1. Authoritarian rulemaking and operationalization Under the general policy framework set by the central and provincial governments, local governments dominated in building regulatory capacity, designing energy saving strategies, and employing multiple policy instruments. Local rulemaking and operationalization follows the institutional logic and the model of authoritarian environ- mentalism, with a clear overarching goal of energy saving; direct, hierarchical decisionmaking inside the govern- ment; and little involvement of the target group of regulated ﬁrms or the general public. Regulatory capacity for energy efﬁciency was strengthened at the local level with strong political power and coordination. All four municipal governments formed leading groups on energy saving or both energy saving and emissions reduction. The common members included (but were not limited to) chief ofﬁcers from the Economic and Information Technology Commission (EITC), the Development and Reform Commission, the Environmental Protection Bureau, the Housing and Urban-rural Development Commission, the Science and Technology Bureau, the Transportation Bureau, the Land and Resources Bureau, and bureaus covering statistics, ﬁnance, state and local taxation, and education, as well as the propaganda department of the Party Commission. Within each of the four municipalities, there are multiple counties and districts under municipal jurisdiction. These lower level governments were also required by the municipal governments to form similar leading groups. While there was an energy saving division in the EITC dedicated to regular management of industrial energy sav- ing, the leading group was considered by the government interviewees to be of paramount signiﬁcance: the lead- ing group helped to prioritize energy saving in the local policy agenda, build political power for enforcement, coordinate between administrative bodies, and consolidate ﬁscal strength and human resources.1 Because of the broad involvement of government ofﬁcials from different agencies, favoritism toward speciﬁc ﬁrms according to personal or organizational connections, if any, was greatly mitigated.2 Moving toward implementation, each of the four municipal governments designed its own energy saving plans and strategies, not through negotiation with local industries but rather through a combination of following central and provincial plans and incorporating local knowledge. Some strategies varied across municipalities, reﬂecting regulators’ knowledge about the status of local industries and potential directions for energy saving.3 For example, industrial structural change was prioritized in some municipalities with higher shares of energy- intensive industries,4 while improving the management of energy use in large ﬁrms was the main focus of muni- cipalities with greater shares of energy consumption from a few major energy consumers.5 Some other strategies were commonly shared, following the priorities in central or provincial plans. One example is the setup of targets and responsibilities: municipal energy saving targets were announced, often more stringent than the ones negotiated with the provincial government to make sure the negotiated ones could be reached; the targets were required to be further disaggregated to lower level governments and to be part of the performance evaluation of the governments and their leaders. Another example is the emphasis on procedures for ﬁrm-level energy statistics and reporting, which led to the establishment of a center for energy conservation supervision in each of the four municipalities.6 Local authorities not only built strong regulatory capacity and identiﬁed strategies and measures, but also actively used multiple policy instruments and were deeply involved in the implementation processes. Table 4 pre- sents detailed policy instruments where enforcement was found to be effective in facilitating decisionmaking or shaping the choices of energy saving in a given ﬁrm. While many of the policies applied generally to most of the ﬁrms, they were not listed if they were not considered important by the interviewees for their ﬁrms’ energy sav- ing. Examples include: (i) an energy saving agreement that was easily fulﬁlled with the targeted saving far below the actual saving; (ii) investment subsidies found to be trivial to the total investment and their availability not affecting ﬁrms’ decision to invest; and (iii) energy efﬁciency standards that were below ﬁrms’ performance, even without energy saving. The policies are presented in Table 4 in a generally decreasing order of active government involvement from left to right, and grouped as mandatory, voluntary, or incentive-based. The strongest tools of local government were the elimination of outdated production facilities and a capacity cap for a given industry in a region. Both effectively disabled a ﬁrm’s facilities or prevented a ﬁrm’s entry with an energy-intensive production process. Indeed, most of the ﬁrms invested in new plants and equipment not because of direct requirements, but because of the concern of ineligibility under stricter elimination standards in the future, or the risk of being suspended to save the production capacity for new, more efﬁcient ﬁrms to enter. The local governments also scheduled electricity quotas and rolling blackouts in advance (see e.g. Fisher-Vanden et al. 2012 for the general background), which caused suspension of part or all of a ﬁrm’s production facilities during a short period. The advanced schedules gave ﬁrms incentives to invest in electricity saving technologies to make sure that their facilities could still operate with reduced electricity consumption, or as bargaining power with the govern- ments to incur fewer blackouts in the future. Responsiveness was manifested most clearly in the process of enforcing the strongest mandatory policies: (i) eliminating outdated facilities and maintaining local capacity caps, and (ii) setting electricity quotas and blackouts.
